attributes.stem:
A 30-year-old woman presents to her General Practice clinic with a 12-month history of recurrent right iliac fossa pain. Each episode lasts for about three days and is associated with abdominal distension and constipation before spontaneous relief of symptoms. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications apart from the oral contraceptive pill.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her abdominal and pelvic examinations are normal. Her BMI is 22 kg/m2. Various laboratory investigations are arranged. Her faecal calprotectin returns a result of 600 μg/g (<50).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with symptoms suggestive of inflammatory bowel disease, particularly Crohn's disease affecting the terminal ileum. The elevated faecal calprotectin (600 μg/g) strongly indicates the presence of intestinal inflammation, warranting further investigation.

Colonoscopy is the most appropriate initial investigation in this case. It allows direct visualisation of the colon and terminal ileum, which is the most common site of Crohn's disease involvement. Colonoscopy also provides the opportunity to obtain biopsies for histological confirmation of the diagnosis. It can assess the extent and severity of inflammation, identify strictures, and rule out other conditions such as ulcerative colitis or colorectal cancer. A colonoscopy might be contra-indicated in acute circumstances, where there is acute inflammation and the risk of perforation - but that is not the case here.

MR enterography is an excellent imaging modality for assessing small bowel involvement in Crohn's disease. It provides detailed images of the bowel wall and surrounding tissues without radiation exposure. However, it is typically performed after colonoscopy to evaluate the extent of disease and detect complications such as fistulas or abscesses that may not be visible during endoscopy.

CT enterography offers similar benefits to MR enterography but involves radiation exposure. For this reason, MRI is generally preferred, especially in young patients who may require repeated imaging over time.

Abdominal ultrasound can be useful in assessing bowel wall thickness and detecting complications of Crohn's disease. However, it is operator-dependent and may not provide as comprehensive information as colonoscopy or cross-sectional imaging. It is more commonly used for follow-up in established Crohn's disease rather than initial diagnosis.

Wireless capsule endoscopy can be valuable for assessing small bowel mucosa beyond the reach of standard endoscopy. However, it is contraindicated if there is a suspicion of strictures or obstruction, which could lead to capsule retention. Additionally, it does not allow for tissue sampling. Therefore, it is not the most appropriate initial investigation in this case.

In summary, colonoscopy is the most appropriate initial investigation for suspected Crohn's disease, allowing direct visualisation and biopsy of the colon and terminal ileum. Depending on the findings, further investigations such as MR enterography may be warranted to fully assess the extent of disease involvement.
learningPoints: Colonoscopy is the most appropriate initial investigation for suspected Crohn's disease, allowing direct visualisation and biopsy of the colon and terminal ileum.
Faecal calprotectin is a useful screening test for inflammatory bowel disease, with levels >50 μg/g warranting further investigation.
MR enterography is preferred over CT enterography for assessing small bowel involvement in Crohn's disease due to lack of radiation exposure.
references: Seyedian SS, Nokhostin F, Malamir MD. A review of the diagnosis, prevention, and treatment methods of inflammatory bowel disease. J Med Life. 2019 Apr-Jun;12(2):113-122. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6685307/
Maffè GC, Brunetti L, Formagnana P, Corazza GR. Ultrasonographic findings in Crohn’s disease. J Ultrasound. 2015;18(1):37-49. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4353828/
Calabrese E. Bowel Ultrasound for the Assessment of Crohn's Disease. Gastroenterol Hepatol (NY). 2011;7(2):107-109. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3061014/
